clinical_history: 52 year-old man transferred by ambulance. The ECG is diagnostic of which one of the following conditions?
diagnosis:
This ECG shows sinus rhythm at about 90 bpm with acute ST elevations in leads I, aVL and V2-V6. These striking antero-lateral ST elevations are due to an extensive current of injury and the pattern is technically known as a monophasic current of injury. Reciprocal ST depressions are present in leads III and aVF. Incipient Q waves are present in leads V3-V6. Borderline left axis deviation is present, with minuscule r waves in III and aVF and a small Q in II, raising the possibility of prior inferior infarction.

The accompanying tall T waves are so-called hyperacute T waves. In housestaff parlance, the term tombstone pattern is often used (but not encouraged) in this context, due to the distinctive shape and high amplitude of the ST-T elevations and the life-threatening clinical implications.

This patient was immediately transferred to the cardiac catheterization laboratory with cardiogenic shock syndrome. He underwent primary angioplasty of a 99% mid-left anterior descending lesion and an intra-aortic balloon pump was placed. An initial echocardiogram showed a markedly reduced left ventricular ejection fraction of 20-25%.
